text.title: Messaging House Template Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Messaging House Template application MUST enforce, plus a skip-this-methodology fallback. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: House MUST have exactly one root value statement at the top. Multiple root lines are rejected.
rule.rationale: Multiple roots collapse the structure into a positioning grab-bag; the house exists to enforce one.

rule.statement: House MUST have exactly 3 pillars supporting the root line. 2 or 5 pillars are rejected.
rule.rationale: Three is the documented cognitive floor for memorability; the closed constraint forces prioritisation.

rule.statement: Each pillar MUST carry 2-4 proof points; every proof point MUST cite evidence (data / quote / asset). Empty proof points are rejected.
rule.rationale: Un-evidenced proof points are marketing wishlist; downstream copy collapses without backing.

rule.statement: Each pillar MUST produce per-channel variants (X tweet, LinkedIn post, email subject, ad copy, landing-page hero). Pillars without variants are rejected.
rule.rationale: Without variants the house stays abstract; concrete variants are what channel teams actually consume.

rule.statement: House MUST have a single named owner accountable for updates. Group-owned houses are rejected.
rule.rationale: Group-owned artefacts decay; named ownership creates an update ritual.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition / triggered skip, and route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Methodology hygiene: mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
